epigraph.text:
The earth is the Lord's, and everything in it, the 
world, and all who live in it.

content.text:
When we want to show how parts make up a whole, a **circle graph** 
(also called a **pie chart**) is often the best choice. The circle 
represents the total, and each "slice" shows what fraction of the whole 
belongs to each category.

A circle graph divides a circle into sectors (wedges), where each sector 
represents a category. The size of each sector shows what percentage of 
the whole that category represents.

content.title: Circle Graph
content.content:
A **circle graph** (or **pie chart**) 
displays data as sectors of a circle where:

- The entire circle represents 100\% (the whole).

- Each sector represents a category's percentage of the whole.

- The central angle of each sector is proportional to its 
 percentage.

- All sectors together fill the entire circle.

content.title: Reading a circle graph
content.problem:
The circle graph shows how a student spends a typical 24-hour day.

center
tikzpicture
 
 33/Sleep (8 hrs),
 25/School (6 hrs),
 17/Homework (4 hrs),
 13/Activities (3 hrs),
 12/Other (3 hrs)
 
tikzpicture
center

1. What activity takes the most time?

2. What percentage of the day is spent on school?

3. How many hours are spent on homework?
content.solution:
1. **Sleep** takes the most time (33\% of the day, or 8 hours).

2. School takes **25\%** of the day.

3. Homework takes 17\% of 24 hours: $0.17 \times 24 \approx 
 \textbf{4 hours}$.

content.text:
Since a circle graph represents 100\%, we often need to convert raw data 
to percentages before creating the graph.

content.title: Converting Data to Percentages
content.content:
To find each category's percentage:

1. Find the total of all values.

2. Divide each category's value by the total.

3. Multiply by 100 to convert to a percent.
\[
\text{Percentage} = \frac{\text{Category value}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\%
\]

content.title: Converting to percentages
content.problem:
A survey asked 200 students about their favorite fruit:

| Fruit | Number of Students |
| Apples | 60 |
| Bananas | 50 |
| Oranges | 40 |
| Grapes | 30 |
| Other | 20 |

Convert each to a percentage.

content.text:
A full circle has 360°. To draw a circle graph accurately, we convert 
each percentage to a central angle.

content.title: Central Angle
content.content:
The **central angle** of a sector is the 
angle at the center of the circle. To find it:
\[
\text{Central angle} = \frac{\text{Percentage}}{100} \times 360°
\]
Or equivalently:
\[
\text{Central angle} = \frac{\text{Category value}}{\text{Total}} \times 360°
\]

content.title: Finding central angles
content.problem:
Using the fruit survey data from the previous example, find the central 
angle for each category.

content.title: Creating a Circle Graph
content.content:
1. Convert each category to a percentage.

2. Calculate the central angle for each category.

3. Draw a circle and mark the center.

4. Use a protractor to draw each sector, starting from the top 
 (12 o'clock position).

5. Label each sector with its category name and percentage.

6. Add a title.

content.text: Circle graphs make it easy to see:

items: Which category is largest or smallest
How categories compare to each other
Whether categories are roughly equal or very different

content.title: Interpreting a budget
content.problem:
The circle graph shows a family's monthly budget.

center
tikzpicture
 
 30/Housing,
 20/Food,
 15/Transportation,
 10/Savings,
 10/Utilities,
 15/Other
 
tikzpicture
center

If the family's monthly income is \$4,500, find the dollar amount 
for each category.

content.title: Working backward from a circle graph
content.problem:
A circle graph shows that 25\% of a company's employees work in 
Sales. If there are 42 employees in Sales, how many employees does 
the company have in total?
content.solution:
Let $T$ = total employees.

We know: $25\% \times T = 42$

So: $0.25T = 42$

Divide both sides by 0.25:
\[
T = \frac{42}{0.25} = 168
\]

The company has **168 employees** in total.

items: You want to show parts of a whole
You have a small number of categories (typically 5 or fewer)
You have many categories (hard to read)
You want to compare values over time (use a line graph)
The parts don't add up to 100\%

content.title: The First Pie Chart
content.content:
William Playfair, who invented the bar graph and line graph, also 
created the first pie chart in 1801. He used it to show the 
proportions of the Turkish Empire located in different continents. 
Playfair called it a "circle diagram." The name "pie chart" came 
later, from the resemblance to a sliced pie.

marginNotes.text:
Just as a body, though one, has 
many parts... so it is with Christ.

He sits enthroned above the circle of 
the earth.

marginNotes.author: John Tukey
marginNotes.text:
An approximate answer to the right problem is 
worth a good deal more than an exact answer to an approximate problem.

marginNotes.author: Edward Tufte
marginNotes.text:
Clutter and confusion are not attributes 
of data—they are shortcomings of design.
